narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 32.50, full-time student status, and school deciles of 4th in both Year 1 and Year 3 grades. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated your probability of failing at 49.1%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA, which was the single most influential factor and actually worked in your favor by suggesting a stronger chance of passing the bar compared to what the model expected. However, this positive pull from your GPA was outweighed by other key elements, starting with your family income bracket, which nudged the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Your Year 3 decile rank followed closely, contributing toward a higher risk of failing the bar, with your Year 1 decile having a similar but slightly lesser push in that direction. Even your LSAT score, though below the typical 36.70 average, played a role in tipping the scales toward failure, while your full-time status provided a small counterbalance by leaning toward success. Overall, compared to typical students who pass—who often have higher deciles around 5th-6th, GPAs closer to 3.21, and LSATs near 36.70—your profile fell short in those academic rankings and test performance, despite your solid GPA edge. We understand this rejection feels disappointing, especially given your background and dedication. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average and seeking ways to improve your school decile rankings through stronger performance or additional academic achievements. You might also explore programs that value your full-time commitment and GPA positively. Keep pushing forward—many successful lawyers faced early setbacks like this and turned them into motivation. We're rooting for your next steps.
